input_path: data\papers\Heithoff et al. - A Digital Shadow for Accurate Robot Motion Control Integrating Data with Friction Models.pdf

extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: Implementation of a Digital Twin can leverage Model‑Based Systems Engineering (MBSE) tooling such as SysML (including internal block diagrams and state‑machine extensions) for specifying requirements, functional, logical and physical architectures. Architecture description languages (ADLs) like ACME, AADL, MontiArc (and MontiArc Automaton) or SysML IBDs are used to model components, connectors, interfaces and composition. Behavioural models are often linked with simulation environments such as MathWorks Simulink or other domain‑specific simulators, and executable code may be generated in C++ or Java. Development workflows may follow methodologies such as RFLP (Requirements‑Functions‑Logics‑Physics), OOSEM, SPES or SMArDT to manage abstraction levels and stakeholder integration.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: The Digital Twin described relies on Model‑Based Systems‑Engineering (MBSE) artifacts ranging from high‑level requirement models to low‑level product models. Functional and logical architectures are captured with architecture description languages (ADLs) such as ACME, AADL, MontiArc, and SysML internal block diagrams, which define components, ports, connectors and composition. Behavioural aspects are modelled with state‑machine extensions of SysML (v1.7) or MontiArc Automaton, and are coupled to simulation tools like MathWorks Simulink for physics‑based or analytical analyses. Implementation‑level models may be expressed in general‑purpose code (C++ or Java). In parallel, a digital shadow supplies a “set of contextual data traces and their aggregation and abstraction collected for a specific purpose”, providing the raw sensor and operational data that feed the models. Thus the constellation comprises geometric or structural models (component diagrams), behavioural models (state machines, Simulink models) and analytical models (e.g., physics‑based equations encapsulated in simulation tools), together with aggregated data streams sourced from the physical asset, managed across heterogeneous modeling notations and tools.
extracted_characteristics.life_cycle_stages: The Digital Twin lifecycle follows the MBSE development chain split into abstraction levels: (1) requirements modeling, (2) functional architecture, (3) logical architecture, and (4) product/technical layer. At each stage the twin is represented by corresponding models – requirements captured in SysML requirement diagrams, functional blocks in SysML internal block diagrams or ADL component‑connector graphs, logical behavior encoded in state‑machine or automaton extensions (e.g., MontiArc Automaton), and detailed product models in simulation tools such as Simulink or executable code (C++, Java). Transition between stages is managed by integrating heterogeneous stakeholder models and by mapping components across notations, while governance follows the MBSE practice of traceability across the RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics) methodology.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: The engineering of the Digital Twin relies on Model‑Based Systems Engineering where the twin is constructed using SysML, ACME, AADL, MontiArc and related architecture description languages. Development follows established methodologies such as RFLP, OOSEM, SPES and SMArDT, enabling systematic capture of requirements, functions, logical models and physics. Model artifacts are version‑controlled and exchanged across tools (SysML, Simulink, C++/Java code) to support validation; behavior models may be executed in state‑machine simulators or code generators. Evolution of the twin is handled by extending the logical and physics models and by re‑generating artifacts while maintaining traceability, though explicit quality‑assurance processes are not detailed in the document.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: The Digital Twin adopts several established modeling standards: SysML (OMG), Architecture Description Languages such as ACME, AADL (ASME/OMG), and MontiArc, as well as simulation standards embodied in MathWorks Simulink. These standards provide a common semantics for component‑connector structures, interfaces and behavior description, enabling interoperability between tools and stakeholders. No specific certification or regulatory compliance frameworks are mentioned.

block_1_docs_retrieved:
conclude in Sec. VII.


II. B ACKGROUND

To better understand our approach, we describe relevant
modeling methods, important concepts for DSs, and methods
on how to derive concrete DSs.


_A. Modeling Software and Systems_

To describe the structure and behavior of systems, ModelBased Systems-Engineering (MBSE) uses systems engineering
modeling languages such as SysML [17] during the development phase. Due to the complexity of systems, modeling
is typically split into different levels of abstraction, e.g.,
requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and
product/technical layer. There exist different methodologies
on how to handle this complexity, e.g., RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics) [18], [19], OOSEM [20],
SPES [21], or SMArDT [22]. To describe the functional
and logical architecture, typically architecture description languages (ADLs) are used. As main concepts, they describe
systems from a component & connector perspective, e.g.,
ACME [23], AADL [24], MontiArc [25], [26] or SysML
IBDs [17], [27]. These typically describe the system’s components, their interfaces, their interconnections, and composition.
Some ADLs also include methods to describe the system’s
behavior, e.g., MontiArc Automaton or state machines in
SysML v1.7. To describe behavior in more detail, these models
are often combined with further (modeling) tools such as
MathWorks Simulink [28], other simulation tools and methods [29], [30], or code in general-purpose languages like C++
or Java.

Thus, a comprehensive description of a system from an
MBSE perspective might have to cope with models in different
modeling notations and different tools. In addition, various
heterogeneous stakeholders and perspectives need to be integrated [31].


_B. Digital Shadow Reference Model_

Kritzinger et al. [32] differentiate digital shadows from
digital twins based on their communication flow. A digital
shadow is characterized by “ _an automated one-way data_
_flow between the state of an existing physical object and_
_a digital object_ [32]”. In practical terms, this means that a
digital shadow functions as a passive entity: it neither operates
autonomously nor pushes data back to the physical system.
Therefore, software systems require active components to
interpret and process this incoming data [33], [34]. A Digital
Twin (DT) can provide such active components [35]–[37].
In the remainder of this paper, we understand a digital
shadow as _“a set of contextual data traces and their ag-_
_gregation and abstraction collected for a specific purpose_



**Fig. 1:** Digital shadow reference model as a UML class
diagram. It defines important concepts for digital shadows in
manufacturing.
